benchmark_description: Compare the observations made by different groups using the same tools and be able to communicate the findings of the investigation.

lesson.title: Comparing What We Found
lesson.intro: Imagine two groups both measured how tall their bean plants grew. Did they get the same answer? Scientists always check each other's work! Comparing results helps everyone learn more and find out if something went wrong.
lesson.body_md:
## Why Compare Results?

When different groups investigate the **same question** using the **same tools**, their results should be close. Comparing results helps us:
- Check if our measurements are correct
- Find out if one group made a mistake
- Get a more complete picture of what really happens

## Using the Same Tools

If Group A uses a ruler and Group B uses their hand, the results are hard to compare. Scientists always use the **same tools**.

| Tool | What It Measures |
|------|------------------|
| Ruler | Length or height |
| Thermometer | Temperature |
| Balance | Mass |
| Cup | Volume of liquid |

## Communicating Findings

**Communicating** means sharing what you found — out loud, in a table, in a drawing, or on a chart. When you share clearly, others can understand and check your work.

## What If Results Are Different?

Sometimes groups get different numbers. Check the measurements again. Look for mistakes. Try the investigation one more time. Small differences are normal. Big differences mean someone needs to check their work.
lesson.worked_example_md:
## Worked Example

**How long does an ice cube take to melt?**

Group 1: 12 min | Group 2: 13 min | Group 3: 12 min

**Comparing:** All three got very close results. The 1-minute difference is likely because the ice cubes were slightly different sizes.

**Communicating:** 'Our class found that an ice cube takes about 12 to 13 minutes to melt at room temperature. All three groups used the same size cups and the same thermometer. Our results were very close, so we feel confident in our answer.'

practice_questions.prompt_md: Why should different groups use the **same tools** when investigating the same question?

choices.label: So everyone can decorate their tools the same way
choices.explanation: Decorating tools does not help compare results.

choices.label: So their results can be compared fairly
choices.explanation: Using the same tools means differences come from the investigation, not from different measuring tools.

choices.label: Because tools are expensive and should be shared
choices.explanation: Sharing tools is fine, but the scientific reason is comparability.

choices.label: So one group can copy the other group's work
choices.explanation: Each group should do their own investigation.

practice_questions.explanation_md: Using the same tools ensures results can be fairly compared.

practice_questions.prompt_md: What does it mean to **communicate** your science findings?

choices.label: Keep your results to yourself
choices.explanation: Keeping results secret is the opposite of communicating.

choices.label: Share what you found with others using words, numbers, or pictures
choices.explanation: Communicating means sharing clearly so others can understand and check your work.

choices.label: Change your results to match your partner
choices.explanation: Changing results is dishonest.

choices.label: Forget what you observed
choices.explanation: Forgetting observations makes them useless.

practice_questions.explanation_md: Communicating means sharing results clearly so others can understand and verify your work.

practice_questions.prompt_md: Group A measured a leaf and got 8 cm. Group B measured the same leaf and got 8 cm. What does this tell you?

choices.label: Both groups made a mistake
choices.explanation: Getting the same result is a good sign, not a sign of mistakes.

choices.label: The measurement is likely correct because both groups agreed
choices.explanation: Two groups independently getting the same result gives confidence that 8 cm is accurate.

choices.label: One group copied the other
choices.explanation: It is possible to get the same result independently by measuring accurately.

choices.label: The leaf is probably 16 cm because you add the two results
choices.explanation: You do not add results from measuring the same object.

practice_questions.explanation_md: Matching results from two independent groups are a sign the measurement is accurate.

practice_questions.prompt_md: Group A found a rock had a mass of 50 grams. Group B found 75 grams. What is the BEST next step?

choices.label: Average the two numbers without checking
choices.explanation: A big difference means someone likely made a mistake — averaging is not the right fix.

choices.label: Check if groups used the same balance and re-measure the rock
choices.explanation: A 25-gram difference signals a likely tool error or measuring mistake.

choices.label: Decide Group B is wrong because they got a bigger number
choices.explanation: A bigger number is not automatically wrong.

choices.label: Ignore the difference and pick the result you like
choices.explanation: Ignoring a big difference means your conclusion may be wrong.

practice_questions.explanation_md: A large difference between two groups measuring the same thing signals a possible error. Re-measure carefully using the same tool.

practice_questions.prompt_md: Three groups measured how far a toy car rolled: 45 cm, 46 cm, and 44 cm. What can you say?

choices.label: Their results are all different so the investigation was wrong
choices.explanation: Small differences (1-2 cm) are normal and expected.

choices.label: Their results are very close, showing the investigation was done carefully
choices.explanation: Results of 44-46 cm are only 1-2 cm apart — small, expected variation showing careful measurement.

choices.label: The car traveled exactly 45 cm every time
choices.explanation: Each group got slightly different numbers — 45 was just one group's result.

choices.label: They should repeat until all groups get exactly the same number
choices.explanation: Exactly the same result every time is very rare — small variation is expected.

practice_questions.explanation_md: Small differences between groups (1-2 cm) are normal. Close results show the investigation was done carefully.

practice_questions.prompt_md: Which is the BEST way to share your science results with the class?

choices.label: Whisper only to your best friend
choices.explanation: Sharing only with one person does not let the class check or learn from your results.

choices.label: Write your results in a chart and read them aloud
choices.explanation: A chart makes results clear and easy to compare; reading them aloud lets everyone hear the findings.

choices.label: Throw away your data sheet after the investigation
choices.explanation: Throwing away data removes the evidence you worked hard to gather.

choices.label: Make up what your results were
choices.explanation: Making up results is dishonest and ruins the investigation.

practice_questions.explanation_md: The best way to communicate findings is clearly and to everyone — a chart plus verbal explanation is strong scientific communication.

practice_questions.prompt_md: Rosa got 55 degrees C and James got 56 degrees C measuring the same heated water. What MOST LIKELY caused the small difference?

choices.label: One of them is lying
choices.explanation: A 1-degree difference is too small to be dishonesty — it is normal measurement variation.

choices.label: Normal small differences in timing or thermometer reading
choices.explanation: Tiny differences (1 degree) are expected from slight differences in when they read or where they placed the thermometer.

choices.label: They used completely different tools so results cannot be compared
choices.explanation: A 1-degree difference does not mean they used different tools.

choices.label: The water temperature changed between readings
choices.explanation: While possible, a 1 degree C difference is better explained by normal measurement variation.

practice_questions.explanation_md: Small differences of 1 degree are normal. They come from tiny variations in how or when a measurement was taken.

practice_questions.prompt_md: Group 1 measured rain with a ruler: 3 cm. Group 2 used a stick marked in inches: about 1 inch. Are these easy to compare?

choices.label: Yes, because both groups measured the same rain
choices.explanation: Measuring the same thing in different units makes direct comparison difficult.

choices.label: No, because they used different units — cm and inches are not the same
choices.explanation: 1 inch equals about 2.54 cm. Different units require conversion before comparing.

choices.label: Yes, because 3 and 1 are both numbers
choices.explanation: Numbers in different units cannot be directly compared.

choices.label: No, because rain cannot be measured
choices.explanation: Rain is measured every day by weather stations around the world.

practice_questions.explanation_md: Different units make direct comparison hard — this is why using the same tools and units matters.

practice_questions.prompt_md: Group 3's result is very different from the other three groups. What should the class do with Group 3's data?

choices.label: Include it without question because all data matters equally
choices.explanation: An outlier very different from all others should be investigated before being included.

choices.label: Investigate whether Group 3 made a measuring error before deciding what to do
choices.explanation: An outlier often signals a mistake. Check Group 3's process and possibly re-measure before deciding.

choices.label: Delete it immediately because it makes the class look bad
choices.explanation: Deleting data just because it is different is dishonest.

choices.label: Award Group 3 a prize for being different
choices.explanation: An outlier is a signal to investigate further, not a prize.

practice_questions.explanation_md: Outliers should trigger an investigation. Check for measuring errors first, then decide whether to include or exclude.

practice_questions.prompt_md: Class A's bean seeds sprouted in 5 days on average; Class B's in 8 days. Both used the same water and sunlight amounts but were in different rooms. What is MOST LIKELY responsible?

choices.label: One class did not water their seeds
choices.explanation: Both classes gave the same amount of water.

choices.label: The rooms had different temperatures, which affected sprouting speed
choices.explanation: Room temperature is a variable that was NOT controlled. Warmer rooms speed up sprouting.

choices.label: Class B forgot to use a ruler
choices.explanation: The question says both classes used the same rulers — measuring tools are not the issue.

choices.label: Seeds always sprout in exactly 5 or 8 days depending on their color
choices.explanation: Seed color does not determine sprouting time. Temperature and moisture are the main factors.

practice_questions.explanation_md: When two groups follow the same procedure but get different results, look for an uncontrolled variable. Room temperature differs between classrooms and is a known factor in sprouting speed.

mastery_check.prompt_md: Four groups measured the same sunflower: 42, 43, 42, 41 cm. What is the BEST conclusion?

choices.label: The sunflower is exactly 42 cm because that is most common
choices.explanation: The best conclusion covers the range of all results.

choices.label: The sunflower is about 41 to 43 cm based on the close results from all groups
choices.explanation: Results ranging 41-43 cm show the sunflower is approximately that range.

choices.label: The height cannot be determined
choices.explanation: Four close measurements give plenty of information.

choices.label: The sunflower grows taller every time it is measured
choices.explanation: Measuring does not make things grow.

mastery_check.explanation_md: Close results from multiple groups show the true value is within that range.

mastery_check.prompt_md: Why is it important to **write down** your results during an investigation instead of just remembering them?

choices.label: Because writing is good handwriting practice
choices.explanation: Handwriting practice is not the scientific reason.

choices.label: Because written records are accurate and can be shared and compared with other groups
choices.explanation: Written records preserve exact numbers so they can be shared, compared, and checked.

choices.label: Because the teacher will take them away at the end
choices.explanation: Teachers use student data to help learning, but that is not the main scientific reason.

choices.label: Because memories are always perfectly accurate
choices.explanation: Memories fade and change over time — written records stay exact.

mastery_check.explanation_md: Written records preserve exact data that can be shared, compared, and checked for accuracy.

mastery_check.prompt_md: Which tool would you use to **compare** how heavy two rocks are?

choices.label: A ruler
choices.explanation: A ruler measures length, not mass.

choices.label: A balance
choices.explanation: A balance measures mass and can show which object is heavier or compare exact masses.

choices.label: A thermometer
choices.explanation: A thermometer measures temperature, not mass.

choices.label: A hand lens
choices.explanation: A hand lens magnifies objects — it does not measure mass.

mastery_check.explanation_md: A balance is the correct tool for comparing mass.

remediation.prompt_md: What tool would you use to measure how long a worm is?

choices.label: A thermometer
choices.explanation: A thermometer measures temperature, not length.

choices.label: A ruler
choices.explanation: A ruler measures length — perfect for measuring a worm.

choices.label: A cup
choices.explanation: A cup measures liquid volume, not length.

choices.label: A balance
choices.explanation: A balance measures mass, not length.

remediation.explanation_md: A ruler measures length. To measure a worm, place a ruler beside it and read the number.

remediation.prompt_md: Group A got 10 cm and Group B got 10 cm measuring the same plant. What does this tell you?

choices.label: Both groups are wrong
choices.explanation: Getting the same result is a good sign.

choices.label: The plant is probably 10 cm tall
choices.explanation: Two groups getting the same result gives confidence that 10 cm is correct.

choices.label: The plant is 20 cm because you add the results
choices.explanation: You do not add results from measuring the same object.

choices.label: You need to measure a third time to know for sure
choices.explanation: Two matching results already give strong confidence.

remediation.explanation_md: When two groups measure the same thing and get the same answer, that result is very likely correct.

remediation.prompt_md: Your class measured how far paper airplanes flew. How should you share your group's result?

choices.label: Write the number on the board so everyone can see it
choices.explanation: Writing the result where everyone can see communicates your finding clearly.

choices.label: Hide your result so other groups cannot copy
choices.explanation: Hiding results is not scientific — sharing allows everyone to learn and compare.

choices.label: Erase it right away
choices.explanation: Erasing data removes the evidence you worked hard to gather.

choices.label: Make up a bigger number so your group looks good
choices.explanation: Making up numbers is dishonest.

remediation.explanation_md: Scientists communicate results by sharing them clearly — writing on the board lets everyone compare your finding.

remediation.prompt_md: Group A used a big ruler and Group B used a tiny ruler, both measuring in centimeters. Will their results be easy to compare?

choices.label: No, because one ruler is bigger
choices.explanation: The size of the ruler does not matter as much as the units it measures in.

choices.label: Yes, because both rulers measure in centimeters so the units match
choices.explanation: As long as both use the same units (centimeters), size does not matter — results will be comparable.

choices.label: No, because rulers never give the same result
choices.explanation: Rulers measuring in the same units will give the same measurement for the same object.

choices.label: Yes, because every ruler gives the same answer always
choices.explanation: Rulers using different units (cm vs. inches) would give different numbers for the same length.

remediation.explanation_md: Using the same units is key for comparison. Two centimeter rulers — even different sizes — will give the same measurement for the same object.

remediation.prompt_md: After an investigation, your teacher asks 'What did you find out?' What is the BEST answer?

choices.label: I do not remember what happened
choices.explanation: This is why recording during the investigation is so important.

choices.label: Our group found that the rock sank in 2 seconds, and Group B found 3 seconds
choices.explanation: This answer shares your result clearly and compares it to another group.

choices.label: I think rocks are cool
choices.explanation: An opinion is not a scientific finding.

choices.label: Whatever the teacher said the answer should be
choices.explanation: The answer comes from your own investigation.

remediation.explanation_md: Good scientific communication shares your actual result clearly, and comparing with other groups' results makes it even stronger.
